sections.heading: Rising intonation ↗
sections.body: Rising intonation signals that something is open, unfinished, or invites a response. Yes/No questions rise at the end because the speaker doesn't know the answer. Also used in lists (except the final item) and in question tags when genuinely asking.

sections.heading: Falling intonation ↘
sections.body: Falling intonation signals completion, certainty, or conclusion. Wh- questions (who, what, where, when, why, how) fall — the speaker expects information, not just yes/no. Statements also fall when the speaker is confident.

sections.heading: Intonation and attitude
sections.body: The same words with different intonation can convey very different attitudes. A flat or falling 'really?' sounds skeptical or uninterested. A rising 'really?' sounds surprised and engaged. Intonation carries enormous pragmatic weight in English.

examples.note: List: rise on each item, fall on last

quiz.question: A rising intonation on a question tag means:
options: You're genuinely asking and not sure of the answer
quiz.explanation: Rising intonation on a question tag signals genuine uncertainty — you really want confirmation.
